items.mistake: Treating the likelihood P(x|θ) as a probability distribution over θ
items.fix: Reserve P(x|θ) for how data x score under fixed θ; only after multiplying by P(θ) and normalizing do you obtain P(θ|x).
items.why: Likelihood and posterior live on different axes—confusing them breaks Bayes' arithmetic.
items.example: P(+|θ=0.01) is tiny, but θ itself can still have high posterior if the prior on rare events is tiny and other evidence is weak.

items.mistake: Using a strong prior then ignoring posterior sensitivity
items.fix: Run prior predictive checks and vary hyperparameters (e.g., α, β in Beta) to see how much the posterior moves.
items.why: With n≈10 flips, Beta(50,50) vs Beta(1,1) can yield very different posteriors—stakeholders need to see that range.
items.example: Same 7/10 heads: Beta(1,1) posterior mean ≈0.64; Beta(50,50) mean ≈0.52—report both when the prior is debatable.

items.mistake: Equating posterior probability with frequentist p-values or confidence
items.fix: Interpret P(θ|x) as belief about θ given data and model; do not read it as 'probability the null is true' in NHST language without translation.
items.why: Different inferential frameworks answer different questions; mixing wording causes false certainty.
items.example: P(θ>0|data)=0.95 is a credible statement about θ, not '95% chance this experiment would repeat the same interval.'
